SUMMARY:
# Seismic Building Codes There is an often-repeated saying, "earthquakes don't kill people, buildings do." Although you can’t control the seismic hazard in the community where you live or work, you can influence the most important factor in saving lives and reducing losses from an earthquake: the adoption and enforcement of up-to-date building codes. Unless your community has adopted the latest model building code, including its seismic provisions, new structures in your community will probably not provide the current minimum level of protection from earthquake hazards to you and others who use them. There is no more important factor in reducing a community’s risk from an earthquake than the adoption and enforcement of up-to-date building codes.

# Seismic Building Codes

![world globe](/profiles/femad8_gov/themes/fema_uswds/images/world-icon-black.png)

There is an often-repeated saying, "earthquakes don't kill people, buildings do." Although you can’t control the seismic hazard in the community where you live or work, you can influence the most important factor in saving lives and reducing losses from an earthquake: the adoption and enforcement of up-to-date building codes.

![Brick building with roof destroyed. ](/sites/default/files/photos/fema_unreinforced-masonry-risk.JPG)

### [Unreinforced Masonry Risk Reduction Report](/sites/default/files/documents/fema_wasatch-front-urm-risk-reduction-strategy.pdf)

Unreinforced masonry was a common building material throughout Utah until building codes began requiring reinforcing steel during masonry construction in Utah beginning in the 1970s. There are an estimated 140,000 unreinforced masonry structures throughout Utah including homes, businesses, schools and houses of worship. Published by FEMA and the state of Utah, this report provides local communities with a strategy to significantly reduce the earthquake risk posed by these buildings.

## What Are Building Codes?

Building codes are sets of regulations governing the design, construction, alteration and maintenance of structures. They specify the minimum requirements to adequately safeguard the health, safety and welfare of building occupants.

Rather than create and maintain their own codes, most states and local jurisdictions adopt the model building codes maintained by the [International Code Council (ICC)](https://www.iccsafe.org/). The ICC’s family of International Codes includes:

![alert - info](/profiles/femad8_gov/themes/fema_uswds/images/arrow.svg)

Learn more with the **[Building Codes Toolkit](/emergency-managers/risk-management/building-science/publications/building-codes-toolkit-homeowners-occupants)**.

## What Are Seismic Codes?

Some provisions within the IBC, IRC and IEBC are intended to ensure that structures can adequately resist seismic forces during earthquakes. These seismic provisions represent the best available guidance on how structures should be designed and constructed to limit seismic risk.

Changes or additions to the seismic provisions come from many different sources, including new research results and documentation of performance in past earthquakes. A primary resource is the [2020 NEHRP Provisions Edition Volume I](/sites/default/files/2020-10/fema_2020-nehrp-provisions_part-1-and-part-2.pdf) and [Volume II](/sites/default/files/2020-10/fema_2020-nehrp-provisions_part-3.pdf). FEMA’s companion document [Earthquake Resistant Design Concepts (FEMA P-749)](/sites/default/files/documents/fema_p-749-earthquake-resistant-design-concepts_112022.pdf) provides a nontechnical background explanation.

Adoption of the model codes is uneven across and within states, even in areas with high levels of seismic hazard. Some states and local jurisdictions have adopted the codes but have made amendments or exclusions relating to the seismic provisions.

Other jurisdictions have been slow to adopt the latest code editions. Unless your community has adopted the latest model building code, including its seismic provisions, new structures in your community will probably not provide the current minimum level of protection from earthquake hazards to you and others who use them.

## How Are The Codes Enforced?

Adopting the latest building codes is only part of the solution. Codes must also be effectively enforced to ensure that buildings and their occupants benefit from advances in seismic provisions in the model codes. For the most part, code enforcement is the responsibility of local government building officials who review design plans, inspect construction work and issue building and occupancy permits.

## What About Older Buildings?

![A cracked building ](/sites/default/files/2020-07/fema_building_codes_damage-older_building_photo.jpg)

Damage to older, reinforced concrete building in the 1994 Northridge Earthquake. © 1994 by Peter W. Clark and Regents of the University of California.

Except in certain circumstances, such as when a building is significantly renovated or altered or there is a change in its use that triggers the IBC or IEBC, the code requirements for existing buildings are those that were in effect when the structure was designed and constructed.

Your community probably has many older structures that are not protected against earthquakes. This is because buildings are often used for decades before being replaced or substantially altered.

These existing buildings are the single biggest contributor to seismic risk in the United States today.

## Can We Make These Buildings Safe?

It’s possible to make these buildings more resistant to earthquakes through seismic retrofitting. When dealing with a population of buildings, the first step is to perform a quick survey using [Rapid Visual Screening of Buildings for Potential Seismic Hazards (FEMA 154)](https://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/15212). The next step is to evaluate the building using [Seismic Evaluation of Existing Buildings (ASCE/SEI 31-03)](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/book/10.1061/9780784407592). If the evaluation shows that retrofitting is needed, this should be done using [Seismic Rehabilitation of Existing Buildings (ASCE/SEI 41-06)](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/abs/10.1061/9780784408841.sup). This standard, which is referenced in the IEBC, is based on [Prestandard and Commentary for the Seismic Rehabilitation of Buildings (FEMA 356)](https://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/757). The FEMA publication [Techniques for the Seismic Rehabilitation of Existing Buildings (FEMA 547)](https://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/8564) provides an extensive description of retrofit techniques for strengthening the structural elements of buildings.

Seismic retrofitting of a building must also include steps to better protect non-structural components (suspended ceilings, non-load-bearing walls and utility systems) and building contents (furnishings, supplies, inventory and equipment). [Reducing the Risks of Non-Structural Earthquake Damage (FEMA E-74)](https://www.fema.gov/fema-e-74-reducing-risks-nonstructural-earthquake-damage) was recently updated and is an excellent resource for information on mitigating risk to non-structural components and contents.

Certain types of buildings, such as unreinforced masonry structures, have performed poorly in past earthquakes and are known to be particularly hazardous. Some local governments in high-hazard areas have enacted ordinances mandating that owners evaluate and retrofit these buildings. In most jurisdictions, however, seismic retrofitting remains voluntary.

## How Important Is Seismic Retrofitting ?

Seismic retrofitting of vulnerable structures is critical to reducing risk. It is important for protecting the lives and assets of building occupants and the continuity of their work. On the whole, communities with more retrofitted structures can recover from earthquakes more rapidly.

If you live or work in retrofitted structures, you’re less likely to be injured during an earthquake. After the earthquake, you’re also more likely to have a home and a job to which you can quickly return.

Businesses that use retrofitted buildings are more likely to survive damaging earthquakes and to sustain shorter business interruptions and fewer inventory losses.

FEMA’s [QuakeSmart](https://www.ready.gov/sites/default/files/2020-04/ready-buisiness_quakesmart_toolkit.pdf) program helps businesses identify and address their seismic risks through retrofitting and other earthquake mitigation activities.

## Conclusions

There is no more important factor in reducing a community’s risk from an earthquake than the adoption and enforcement of up-to-date building codes. Evaluating older buildings and retrofitting structural and non-structural components also are critical steps. To survive and remain resilient, communities should also strengthen their core infrastructure and critical facilities so that these can withstand an earthquake or other disaster and continue to provide essential services.

## For More Information

For many years, FEMA has supported seismic code development processes and promoted the adoption and enforcement of seismic codes through its participation in the [National Earthquake Hazards Reduction Program (NEHRP)](/emergency-managers/risk-management/earthquake/nehrp).

FEMA has produced many publications for a variety of audiences to identify and correct building vulnerabilities through seismic rehabilitation. Visit [Earthquake Publications—Building Codes and Seismic Rehabilitation](/emergency-managers/risk-management/building-science/earthquakes) to review these resources.

Visit Seismic Building Code Document Library

Building Codes for Seismic Resilience
=====================================

A Comprehensive Guide to Designing Structures for Earthquake Safety

![Image 3: Sarah Lee](https://s3.amazonaws.com/NumberAnal/image/Sarah_Lee_120.jpg)

Sarah Lee AI generated Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8 7 min read· June 12, 2025

3 views

[![Image 4: white and brown concrete house near trees at daytime](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472377723522-4768db9c41ce?crop=entropy&cs=tinysrgb&fit=max&fm=jpg&ixid=M3w3MDgyMDR8MHwxfHNlYXJjaHwxfHxidWlsZGluZyUyMGNvZGVzJTJDJTIwc2Vpc21pYyUyMHJlc2lsaWVuY2UlMkMlMjBlYXJ0aHF1YWtlJTIwc2FmZXR5fGVufDB8MHx8fDE3NDk3MjkyODV8MA&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&q=80&w=500&?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472377723522-4768db9c41ce?crop=entropy&cs=tinysrgb&fit=max&fm=jpg&ixid=M3w3MDgyMDR8MHwxfHNlYXJjaHwxfHxidWlsZGluZyUyMGNvZGVzJTJDJTIwc2Vpc21pYyUyMHJlc2lsaWVuY2UlMkMlMjBlYXJ0aHF1YWtlJTIwc2FmZXR5fGVufDB8MHx8fDE3NDk3MjkyODV8MA&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&q=80&w=500&?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)

Photo by [eduard](https://unsplash.com/@eduardmilitaru?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)

Earthquakes pose a significant threat to structures and human life. Building codes play a crucial role in ensuring that buildings are designed and constructed to withstand seismic forces. In this article, we will explore the intricacies of building codes and seismic design to create structures that can resist earthquakes.

Seismic Analysis and Design Strategies
--------------------------------------

Seismic analysis is the process of evaluating a structure's response to earthquake-induced forces. It involves understanding the dynamics of the structure and the characteristics of the seismic forces.

### Fundamentals of Seismic Analysis

Seismic analysis is based on the principles of structural dynamics and earthquake engineering. The primary goal is to determine the structure's response to seismic forces, which is influenced by factors such as:

*   The structure's mass, stiffness, and damping properties
*   The characteristics of the earthquake, including its intensity, frequency content, and duration
*   The soil conditions at the site

The equation of motion for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system subjected to seismic excitation is given by:

m x¨(t)+c x˙(t)+k x(t)=−m x¨g(t)m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = -m\ddot{x}_g(t)m x¨(t)+c x˙(t)+k x(t)=−m x¨g​(t)

where m m m is the mass, c c c is the damping coefficient, k k k is the stiffness, x(t)x(t)x(t) is the relative displacement, and x¨g(t)\ddot{x}_g(t)x¨g​(t) is the ground acceleration.

### Design Strategies for Enhancing Seismic Resilience

To enhance seismic resilience, designers employ various strategies, including:

*   **Ductility design**: Allowing the structure to deform inelastically, absorbing seismic energy and reducing the forces transmitted to the structure.
*   **Capacity design**: Ensuring that the structure's capacity to resist seismic forces is greater than the demand imposed by the earthquake.
*   **Seismic isolation**: Decoupling the structure from the ground, reducing the transmission of seismic forces.
*   **Energy dissipation**: Using devices or systems to dissipate seismic energy, reducing the structure's response.

The following flowchart illustrates the seismic design process:

### Role of Ductility and Energy Dissipation

Ductility and energy dissipation play critical roles in seismic design. Ductility allows the structure to deform inelastically, absorbing seismic energy and reducing the forces transmitted to the structure. Energy dissipation devices or systems, such as viscous dampers or hysteretic dampers, can be used to further reduce the structure's response.

The ductility ratio, defined as the ratio of the ultimate deformation to the yield deformation, is a key parameter in seismic design. A higher ductility ratio indicates a greater ability to withstand seismic forces.

Code Provisions for Seismic Design
----------------------------------

Building codes provide guidelines for seismic design, ensuring that structures are designed and constructed to withstand seismic forces. Major building codes, such as the International Building Code (IBC) and the American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) 7, provide provisions for seismic design.

### Overview of Seismic Design Provisions in Major Building Codes

The IBC and ASCE 7 provide a framework for seismic design, including:

*   **Seismic hazard assessment**: Evaluating the seismic hazard at the site, including the site's seismic design category and the design spectral response acceleration.
*   **Structural analysis**: Performing a structural analysis to determine the structure's response to seismic forces.
*   **Design and detailing**: Designing and detailing the structure to resist seismic forces, including requirements for material properties and seismic detailing.

### Requirements for Seismic Detailing and Material Properties

Seismic detailing and material properties play a crucial role in ensuring that structures can withstand seismic forces. Building codes provide requirements for:

*   **Material properties**: Specifying the required material properties, such as strength and ductility.
*   **Seismic detailing**: Providing guidelines for seismic detailing, including reinforcement requirements and connection details.

The following table summarizes the seismic detailing requirements for reinforced concrete structures in the ACI 318 code:

| Seismic Design Category | Minimum Reinforcement Ratio | Maximum Spacing of Transverse Reinforcement |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A | 0.0018 | - |
| B | 0.0033 | m i n(6 d b,6)min(6d_b, 6)min(6 d b​,6) |
| C | 0.005 | m i n(4 d b,4)min(4d_b, 4)min(4 d b​,4) |
| D | 0.007 | m i n(3 d b,3)min(3d_b, 3)min(3 d b​,3) |

### Code Compliance Strategies for Complex Structures

Complex structures, such as irregular or high-rise buildings, require special consideration in seismic design. Code compliance strategies include:

*   **Nonlinear dynamic analysis**: Performing a nonlinear dynamic analysis to evaluate the structure's response to seismic forces.
*   **Peer review**: Conducting a peer review to ensure that the design meets the code requirements and is consistent with current engineering practice.

Advanced Seismic Design Techniques
----------------------------------

Advanced seismic design techniques are being developed and implemented to improve seismic resilience. These techniques include:

### Application of Performance-Based Design

Performance-based design (PBD) is an approach that focuses on achieving specific performance objectives, such as minimizing damage or ensuring life safety. PBD involves:

*   **Defining performance objectives**: Establishing clear performance objectives, such as the desired level of damage or the acceptable probability of collapse.
*   **Evaluating seismic hazard**: Assessing the seismic hazard at the site, including the probability of occurrence of different earthquake scenarios.
*   **Analyzing structural response**: Analyzing the structure's response to seismic forces using advanced analytical techniques, such as nonlinear dynamic analysis.

### Use of Seismic Isolation and Energy Dissipation Systems

Seismic isolation and energy dissipation systems are used to reduce the transmission of seismic forces to the structure. These systems include:

*   **Seismic isolation bearings**: Devices that decouple the structure from the ground, reducing the transmission of seismic forces.
*   **Viscous dampers**: Devices that dissipate seismic energy, reducing the structure's response.
*   **Hysteretic dampers**: Devices that dissipate seismic energy through inelastic deformation.

### Innovative Materials and Their Role in Seismic Resilience

Innovative materials, such as fiber-reinforced polymers (FRP) and shape memory alloys (SMA), are being developed and used to improve seismic resilience. These materials offer advantages such as:

*   **High strength-to-weight ratio**: Allowing for the design of lighter and more efficient structures.
*   **High ductility**: Enabling the structure to deform inelastically without significant loss of strength.
*   **Self-centering capability**: Allowing the structure to return to its original position after an earthquake.

Case Studies in Seismic Design
------------------------------

Case studies provide valuable insights into the application of seismic design principles and the performance of structures during earthquakes.

### Examples of Successful Seismic Design Implementations

Examples of successful seismic design implementations include:

*   **The Transbay Transit Center in San Francisco**: Designed using seismic isolation and energy dissipation systems, this structure has been shown to be highly resilient to seismic forces.
*   **The Tokyo Skytree**: This tower was designed using advanced seismic design techniques, including seismic isolation and wind-resistant design.

### Lessons Learned from Past Earthquakes and Code Revisions

Past earthquakes have provided valuable lessons for seismic design, including:

*   **The importance of ductility and energy dissipation**: The 1994 Northridge earthquake highlighted the importance of ductility and energy dissipation in seismic design.
*   **The need for robust seismic detailing**: The 2010 Chile earthquake demonstrated the importance of robust seismic detailing in ensuring the performance of structures during earthquakes.

Code revisions have also reflected these lessons, with updates to seismic design provisions and requirements for seismic detailing and material properties.

### Future Directions in Seismic Design and Building Codes

Future directions in seismic design and building codes include:

*   **Advancements in performance-based design**: Continuing to develop and refine PBD approaches, including the use of advanced analytical techniques and experimental methods.
*   **Integration of new materials and technologies**: Incorporating innovative materials and technologies, such as FRP and SMA, into seismic design practice.
*   **Improving seismic resilience**: Focusing on improving seismic resilience, including the use of seismic isolation and energy dissipation systems.

References
----------

1.   [American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE). (2022). Minimum Design Loads and Associated Criteria for Buildings and Other Structures (ASCE/SEI 7-22).](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/book/10.1061/9780784415788)
2.   [International Code Council (ICC). (2021). International Building Code (IBC).](https://codes.iccsafe.org/content/IBC2021P1)
3.   [American Concrete Institute (ACI). (2019). Building Code Requirements for Structural Concrete (ACI 318-19).](https://www.concrete.org/publications/internationalconcreteabstractsportal.aspx?m=details&ID=51718843)
4.   [Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA). (2020). Seismic Design and Retrofit of Buildings (FEMA P-751).](https://www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/2020-07/fema_p-751_seismic-design-retrofit-buildings.pdf)

FAQ
---

### What is seismic resilience?

Seismic resilience refers to the ability of a structure to withstand and recover from seismic events.

### What is the role of building codes in seismic design?

Building codes provide guidelines and requirements for seismic design, ensuring that structures are designed and constructed to withstand seismic forces.

### What is performance-based design?

Performance-based design is an approach that focuses on achieving specific performance objectives, such as minimizing damage or ensuring life safety, during seismic events.

### What are some advanced seismic design techniques?

Advanced seismic design techniques include seismic isolation, energy dissipation systems, and the use of innovative materials, such as fiber-reinforced polymers and shape memory alloys.

--- SOURCE 4: Innovative Materials to Improve Bridge Seismic Resilience ---
URL: https://www.structuremag.org/article/innovative-materials-to-improve-bridge-seismic-resilience/

SUMMARY:
Replacing conventional materials in the plastic hinge region with innovative materials can improve the seismic resilience of bridge structures.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.structuremag.org/app/uploads/2024/04/STRUCTURE-black-transparent-01.png)

# **Innovative Materials to Improve Bridge Seismic Resilience**

*Jed Bingle, P.E., S.E.*

![](https://www.structuremag.org/app/uploads/2025/08/318-PLUS-300x250-1.jpg)

The current seismic design philosophy for bridges in high seismic risk areas is prescribed by nationally accepted design standards that have been based on laboratory testing since the 1960s. For ordinary bridges that are expected to experience strong seismic ground motions, this philosophy prescribes structural details that have been shown to provide a low probability of collapse.

To construct a modern, economical bridge that meets this criterion, it is acceptable for elements of the structure to suffer significant damage. As long as the bridge does not collapse and users can safely get off the structure, the design is considered successful. Using conventional construction methods to construct a bridge without significant damage is typically considered to be uneconomical.

The typical energy dissipation mechanism for modern economical multi-span bridges is to allow plastic hinging. For bridges, the plastic hinge is designed to be a short portion of the length of the steel reinforced concrete column, where the column connects to the crossbeam and where the column connects to the foundation.

The plastic hinge mechanism using conventional materials requires permanent damage to elements that are critical to the serviceability and stability of a bridge. A bridge experiencing the ground motions of a design earthquake may experience permanent damage, such as yielding of the longitudinal steel reinforcement in the plastic hinge region and crushing or spalling of the conventional concrete. This type of damage may lead to restricting the types of vehicles that can use the bridge or require complete closure. The damage may require costly repair or replacement of the column, and may even require replacement of the bridge.

With research support from the University of Nevada, Reno (UNR) – one of the top earthquake engineering laboratories in the U.S. – the Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT) has constructed a first real-world pilot project bridge. The project incorporated innovative materials in the plastic hinge region that will not only eliminate the need for permanent damage of the structure but also provide a necessary energy dissipation mechanism.

Replacing conventional materials in the plastic hinge region with innovative materials can improve the seismic resilience of bridge structures. Conventional steel reinforcement can be replaced with a superelastic shape memory alloy (SMA), and conventional concrete can be replaced with engineered cementitious composite (ECC).

## Shape Memory Alloy

Shape memory alloys (SMA) have historically been used in robotics, automotive, biomedical, and aerospace applications. Superelastic shape memory alloys for this type of bridge application are a special type of metal that is manufactured from a combination of nickel and titanium. The metal is shaped into smooth round bars that replace longitudinal steel rebar in the plastic hinge region of bridge columns.

Conventional steel rebar used in bridges will return to its original and undeformed shape if not stressed beyond its yield point. Rebar is sized for strength and service limit states to remain below this limit. If stressed beyond the yield point, conventional rebar will exhibit residual deformation after unloading. Deformation of the conventional rebar beyond the yield point is essential to dissipate energy during a seismic event. The challenge of relying on deformation beyond the yield point is that the structure will not be operational after a seismic event.

For SMA, the alloy will deform like steel beyond the yield point, but it will return to its undeformed shape. This means the alloy is superelastic. Energy can still be dissipated by stretching the SMA and, once the earthquake motions subside, the SMA will return to its original shape.

Superelastic shape memory alloys undergo crystalline structure phase transformations, between the austenite and martensite phases when stressed. When the alloy is loaded in the austenite phase, the material will behave linearly, similar to steel up to the yield point. The alloy will transform into the twinned, martensite phase as loading continues. In the twinned state, the crystalline structure is oriented to allow the microstructure to shear without breaking molecular bonds when stressed. As loading continues beyond the yield point, the martensite will begin to de-twin, allowing the alloy to behave plastically. When unloading follows, the martensite will transform back to austenite and the material recovers to its original undeformed shape.

The SMA is only needed for energy dissipation in the plastic hinge region. Therefore, the SMA is not needed for the entire height of the column or elsewhere in the bridge. As compared to conventional steel rebar, SMA alloys are expensive, so the SMA is only used in the plastic hinge regions of the column. Due to the difficulty of machining this specific alloy, headed couplers have been shown to be the only feasible means to couple the SMA to steel rebar.

## Engineered Cementitious Composite

Engineered cementitious composite (ECC) is a cement-based mixture that is similar to conventional concrete used in modern reinforced columns. A typical ECC mix contains water, cement, fine sand, polyvinyl alcohol fibers, and chemical admixtures.  The key difference between conventional concrete and ECC is that the coarse aggregates are replaced with fibers to provide improved ductile performance.  The inclusion of the fiber gives ECC the ability to have a tensile strain capacity as high as six times that of conventional concrete. The high tensile and compressive strain ductility cause ECC to not spall like conventional concrete.  The ductility, tensile strength, and unique cracking behavior make ECC a much more resilient material compared to conventional concrete.

## First Implementation

Together, these innovative materials work to provide an energy dissipation mechanism similar to that which is incorporated into modern bridges, but will significantly reduce the risk of damage to the structure and eliminate respective repair costs. This will create a bridge that is more likely to remain serviceable after signiﬁcant earthquake ground motions and reduce or eliminate the need for repair or replacement.

M.‘Saiid’ Saiidi, Ph.D., P.E., F.ASCE, at UNR, has performed extensive research and testing to validate the use of these innovative materials in bridges. There have been several levels of testing completed, including small scale testing of the SMA and ECC to establish material properties, testing of individual columns for specific plastic hinge performance, and full-scale multiple-span bridges subjected to earthquake motions from the shake table equipment in the Earthquake Engineering Laboratory at UNR.

WSDOT decided to incorporate SMA and ECC into a new bridge that is part of the State Route 99 Alaskan Way Viaduct Replacement project in the Sodo district of Seattle, WA. The primary overall goal of this mega-project is to replace the seismically damaged and compromised Alaskan Way Viaduct structure with a single-bore tunnel under the core of the city (*See the tunnel article in the January 2019 issue of STRUCTURE*). This bridge is one of many in the vicinity of the south portal of the new tunnel. It will serve as a northbound off-ramp that will span over two roadway alignments providing access to the tunnel.

The bridge is a three-span, 400-foot long structure carrying two lanes of trafﬁc and has a roadway width of 30½ feet. The bridge superstructure consists of two lines of prestressed, precast concrete tub girders. The abutments have a semi-integral end diaphragm and are founded on 8-foot diameter drilled shafts.

The superstructure type and foundation type of this specific bridge is not necessary for the SMA and ECC to improve seismic resilience. Generally, these materials can be used where any conventional plastic hinges are currently being used.

To encourage new research technologies to be incorporated into practical projects, the Federal Highway Administration created the Innovative Bridge and Research Deployment (IBRD) program. This program granted funds in the amount of $400,000 for this WSDOT project. These funds were intended to cover the costs of lab testing at UNR, procuring the innovative materials, and project documentation costs.

After the completion of construction of the bridge, the Transportation Research Board published NCHRP Research Report 864: *Seismic Evaluation of Bridge Columns with Energy Dissipating Mechanisms*. This report provides guidelines for quantitative analysis and design methods and construction procedures to incorporate SMA and ECC in columns.

By incorporating shape memory alloy and engineered cementitious composite in a typical highway bridge, WSDOT has demonstrated that bridges in high seismic regions can be improved upon with a small investment in new innovative materials. Bridges subjected to earthquakes can perform without the need to rely on permanent damage to dissipate energy. This means that new bridges can remain operational and useful after an earthquake without costly repair or replacement.■

# Innovations in Seismic-Resistant Structures: How New Materials are Changing the Game

![Innovations in Seismic-Resistant Structures](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/nsplsh_f0670ddc30224047b99595ab3c0370e6~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_147,h_222,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/nsplsh_f0670ddc30224047b99595ab3c0370e6~mv2.jpg)

## **Introduction**

As seismic activity continues to rise globally, the demand for more resilient infrastructure is becoming increasingly critical. Traditional materials like concrete and steel have been the backbone of structural design, but their limitations, particularly in high-seismic regions, are well-documented ([Fig. 1](https://www.slideshare.net/slideshow/lec02-earthquake-damage-to-concrete-structure-earthquake-engineering-asscprof-nasser-elshafey/79020245)).

In response, the field of seismic-resistant structures is evolving, with innovative materials offering enhanced safety and durability. These fibre-reinforced           advanced materials are specifically engineered to absorb and [dissipate energy during earthquakes](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/10.1061/%28ASCE%29ST.1943-541X.0003428), minimizing structural damage and saving lives.

![Earthquake damage to concrte and steel structures](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_637044c31d8340f4997a1824d1a7bfc4~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_73,h_54,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_637044c31d8340f4997a1824d1a7bfc4~mv2.png)

While current seismic design codes, such as [Eurocodes](https://www.confinedmasonry.org/wp-content/uploads/2009/09/Eurocode-8-1-Earthquakes-general.pdf) [1], [AASHTO](https://www.ce.memphis.edu/7137/PDFs/AASHTO/Roy_Imbsen_9A.pdf) [2], and [Indian standards](https://law.resource.org/pub/in/bis/S03/is.1893.1.2002.pdf) [3], provide robust guidelines, they also highlight the need for better-performing materials in response to extreme loads. This article delves into the materials that have the capability to transform the landscape of seismic-resistant construction ([Fig. 2](https://camhirstrobots.com/camhirst-3dcp-transforming-construction/)), ensuring that our infrastructure is more prepared than ever to face the unpredictability of high magnitude earthquakes. See material list:

[High-performance fiber-reinforced concrete](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/10.1061/%28ASCE%29ST.1943-541X.0003428#body-ref-c30) (HPFRC)

[Engineered cementitious composites](https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/0964-1726/24/2/025024/meta) (ECC)

[3D concrete printing](https://www.structuresinsider.com/post/know-about-3d-printed-concrete) (3DCP)

![A comparison between the tensile behavior of normal concrete, FRC and HPFRCC](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_6aa8ca8218e24e028065407f7e66b707~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_47,h_14,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_6aa8ca8218e24e028065407f7e66b707~mv2.png)

## **Shifting the Paradigm in Structural Design**

Traditional materials like concrete, while durable, have inherent limitations when exposed to seismic forces. Under extreme lateral loads, these materials may exhibit [brittle failure](https://acemrl.engin.umich.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/412/2018/10/Influence-of-Matrix-Ductility-on-Tension-Stiffening-Behavior-of-Steel-Reinforced-Engineered-Cementitious-Composites-ECC.pdf), compromising structural integrity (Fig. 1) [4]. This has led to the development of new materials designed to withstand the unpredictable nature of seismic events.

One such material is HPFRC, which offers improved ductility and crack resistance compared to standard concrete with better aesthetics (Fig. 3a). Another breakthrough is ECC, known for its [strain-hardening](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Work_hardening) capabilities and ability to endure tensile stress without breaking (Fig. 3b and Table 1). Additionally, 3DCP allows for the precise placement of these advanced materials (Fig. 3c), optimizing their performance in seismic zones**.**

![Examples of high-performance material structures](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_4a372adfe4674ec79f73b2ba6b5c92ab~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_47,h_14,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_4a372adfe4674ec79f73b2ba6b5c92ab~mv2.png)

Seismic design codes such as Eurocodes ([EN 1998-1](https://www.confinedmasonry.org/wp-content/uploads/2009/09/Eurocode-8-1-Earthquakes-general.pdf)), AASHTO ([LRFD Bridge Design Specifications](https://www.ce.memphis.edu/7137/PDFs/AASHTO/Roy_Imbsen_9A.pdf)), and Indian Standards ([IS 1893:2016](https://law.resource.org/pub/in/bis/S03/is.1893.1.2002.pdf)) underline the importance of material ductility and energy absorption in seismic designs.

For instance, Eurocode 8 specifies the use of materials with enhanced energy dissipation and deformation capabilities. These codes reinforce the necessity of adopting materials like HPFRC and ECC, which provide higher performance under seismic conditions, as well as construction innovations like 3DCP, which facilitate the use of these materials in complex designs.[[1]](#_msocom_1) [[2]](#_msocom_2) 

## **Material Innovations in Seismic Resistance**

New materials for seismic-resistant structures are developed with specific properties that make them better suited to withstand seismic loads. HPFRC, ECC, and 3DCP stand out for their ductility, meaning they can undergo significant deformation without fracturing (Table 1).

This is a key feature when considering that seismic forces can cause rapid, [unpredictable movements](https://www.structuresinsider.com/post/seismic-behavior-of-buildings-explained). These innovative materials are designed with strain-hardening properties, meaning that after initial cracking, they can continue to deform under stress, increasing their load-bearing capacity.

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Material** | **Fiber types** | **Compressive strength (MPa)** | **Tensile strength (MPa)** | **Tensile strain capacity (%)** | **Reference** |
| **ECC** | PVA, PE, or PP fibers | 20–95 | 2–6 | 1–8 | [Yi (2022)](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/10.1061/%28ASCE%29ST.1943-541X.0003428#core-c71) [5] |
| **HPFRC** | Hybrid (PVA and steel) fibers | 40–45 | 1–3 | 0.1–0.5 | [Jen et al. (2016)](https://ascelibrary.org/doi/10.1061/%28ASCE%29ST.1943-541X.0003428#core-c53) [6] |

**Table 1.** Summary of major high-performance materials

Another important property is [self-healing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0950061817317749#:~:text=Engineered%20Cementitious%20Composite%20%28ECC%29%20normally%20contains%20large%20amount,%CE%BCm%29%2C%20it%20is%20considered%20of%20high%20self-healing%20potential.), which is a feature of some modern cementitious composites such as ECC (Fig. 4) [8]. This means that small cracks, which typically propagate in traditional concrete under stress can close naturally over time, preserving the material’s integrity.

![Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope (ESEM) images showing self-healing ECC within cracks in specimens ](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_2c882aca61394e049959b282108c4467~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_75,h_27,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_2c882aca61394e049959b282108c4467~mv2.png)

These innovative materials are also notable for their superior energy absorption, which allows them to dissipate seismic forces before they cause major structural damage. For example, 3D printed lattice structures using advanced cementitious composites can [absorb and redistribute more seismic energy](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384296995_Effect_of_strain_hardening_material_on_seismic_performance_of_3D_printed_concrete_wall_a_numerical_study) across the structure, reducing local stress concentrations compared to conventional concrete ([Fig. 5](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384296995_Effect_of_strain_hardening_material_on_seismic_performance_of_3D_printed_concrete_wall_a_numerical_study)).

![Seismic behaviour of 3DCP lattice wall compared to conventional concrete solid wall](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_934d9429977c4f95aa81c974f0ddb0b2~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_124,h_83,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_934d9429977c4f95aa81c974f0ddb0b2~mv2.png)
![Seismic behaviour of 3DCP lattice wall compared to conventional concrete solid wall](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_6ddd2d7d5ea14927805c4963c25bf0c4~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_90,h_74,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_6ddd2d7d5ea14927805c4963c25bf0c4~mv2.png)

In addition to these innovations, the incorporation of steel fibers into concrete matrices has significantly [improved the tensile strength](https://www.structuresinsider.com/post/advantages-of-using-steel-fibre-reinforced-concrete-instead-of-rebar) and post-cracking behavior of seismic-resistant walls and columns. This advancement offers a more comprehensive approach to ensuring that critical infrastructure can survive intense seismic activity with minimal damage.

## **Case Studies of Innovative Material Applications**

Several notable case studies illustrate the real-world application of these new materials in seismic-resistant structures:

1.     [**Rokko Island in Japan**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rokk%C5%8D_Island)**:** This artificial island, constructed using ECC for its earthquake-resistant seawalls, has proven to be highly resilient. During a recent seismic event, the ECC structures exhibited minimal damage, while nearby conventional concrete structures suffered significant cracks and deterioration.

![Rokko island in Japan](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_a51475ab80b44bdd928ef522bb2f1416~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_90,h_24,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_a51475ab80b44bdd928ef522bb2f1416~mv2.png)

2.     [**Mexico City’s Torre Reforma**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torre_Reforma)**:** As one of the tallest buildings in a highly seismic area, the Torre Reforma incorporates HPFRC in its core structure. This allowed for greater flexibility and energy dissipation during the 2017 earthquake of [7.1 magnitude](https://www.bbc.com/news/world-latin-america-41332012),[[3]](#_msocom_3) [[4]](#_msocom_4) [[SF5]](#_msocom_5)  outperforming neighboring buildings constructed with traditional concrete.

![Torre Reforma in Mexico city](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_addeddd4face4fa4851bd01912ea9f97~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_86,h_30,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_addeddd4face4fa4851bd01912ea9f97~mv2.png)

3.     [**The 3D printed seismic house in Guatemala**](https://newatlas.com/architecture/progreso-3d-printed-house/)**:** Using 3DCP materials, the prototype dwelling has been designed to withstand local seismic activity and combines modern 3DCP techniques with traditional local craftsmanship in the form of a thatched roof. It showed a significant increase in ductility and energy absorption compared to conventional methods, proving the efficacy of 3DCP in seismic applications. The unnamed house was built using COBOD's BOD2 printer, which was also used on the [recent post office in India](https://newatlas.com/architecture/3d-printed-post-office-india-cobod/?itm_source=newatlas&itm_medium=article-body) and Europe's first [two-story 3D-printed house](https://newatlas.com/architecture/kamp-c-3d-printed-house/?itm_source=newatlas&itm_medium=article-body).

![World's largest 3D-printed building in Dubai](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_f00fa03d3a7f463cb7054d2741b987c3~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_47,h_12,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_f00fa03d3a7f463cb7054d2741b987c3~mv2.png)

## **Challenges in Adoption**

Despite the promise of these new materials, there are challenges to their widespread adoption. The cost of advanced materials like HPFRC and ECC is higher than traditional concrete, which can be a barrier in large-scale projects. Additionally, the availability of specialized materials and the equipment required for 3DCP can be limited in certain regions.

Moreover, these innovations demand specialized knowledge for proper application, meaning that contractors and engineers must undergo training to ensure correct usage. One notable example is the use of UHPC in the [Perez Art Museum in downtown Miami](https://in.hotels.com/go/usa/perez-art-museum-miami). Built on Biscayne Bay, the museum faces challenges from sea air, salt exposure, and the risk of tropical storms and hurricanes.

UHPC was employed to produce 100 16-foot-long mullions, supporting the world’s largest impact-resistant window at the time ([Fig. 9](https://in.hotels.com/go/usa/perez-art-museum-miami)). This demonstrates the potential but also the complexity of using advanced materials in construction.  

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_9af76c73266449d48602784a661dbd36~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_375,h_250,al_c,blur_30/a08c91_9af76c73266449d48602784a661dbd36~mv2.png)
![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_9af76c73266449d48602784a661dbd36~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_488,h_325,fp_0.50_0.50/a08c91_9af76c73266449d48602784a661dbd36~mv2.png)
![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_0cc1ebba0d8240bd92e09021f14882d5~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_375,h_250,al_c,q_30,blur_30/a08c91_0cc1ebba0d8240bd92e09021f14882d5~mv2.jpg)
![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_0cc1ebba0d8240bd92e09021f14882d5~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_488,h_325,fp_0.50_0.50,q_90/a08c91_0cc1ebba0d8240bd92e09021f14882d5~mv2.jpg)

**Fig. 9.** Perez Art Museum, Downtown Miami Miami City,USA (Source: [Miami New Times](https://in.hotels.com/go/usa/perez-art-museum-miami))

![Comparison of the conventional concrete to high performance innovative materials](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_2c82875d9cd743db836346e174b4dc39~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_81,h_29,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_2c82875d9cd743db836346e174b4dc39~mv2.png)

However, ongoing research and development are addressing these concerns, with efforts to lower production costs and improve material accessibility. As the industry becomes more familiar with these technologies, the integration of advanced materials into seismic-resistant designs is expected to become more commonplace.

## **Future Directions in Seismic-Resistant Materials**

Looking forward, seismic-resistant materials are set to undergo even more groundbreaking advancements. Emerging smart materials (Fig. 10), capable of adapting in real-time to seismic forces, represent a cutting-edge solution. These materials are engineered to alter properties like stiffness, damping, [flexibility and shape retention](https://www.bigrentz.com/blog/earthquake-proof-buildings) in response to external conditions, offering dynamic earthquake resistance. Examples of such innovative materials include:

[**Blue**](http://1.Blue) [**Mussels:**](https://journals.ekb.eg/article_361437_e14da723878053bd9c0e76c8cb49be1c.pdf) Blue mussels anchor to rocks using adhesive byssal threads. Researchers are exploring how their flexibility can improve building resilience ([Fig. 10a](https://journals.ekb.eg/article_361437_e14da723878053bd9c0e76c8cb49be1c.pdf)).

[**Shape Memory Alloys:**](https://designhorizons.org/innovative-earthquake-resistant-materials-for-modern-buildings/) Shape memory alloys represent a fascinating advancement in materials science, particularly for their application in earthquake-resistant construction ([Fig. 10b](https://designhorizons.org/innovative-earthquake-resistant-materials-for-modern-buildings/)).

[**BuildBlock ICF Blocks:**](https://buildblock.com/wp-content/uploads/Hardwall-render-Concrete-Half-Length-Composite.png)This high-performance [insulating concrete form](https://buildblock.com/products/) (ICF) wall system consists of two 2 ½ inch Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) panels, connected by durable, high-density plastic webs, Its innovative design enhances structural integrity, making it ideal for earthquake-resistant construction ([Fig. 10c](https://buildblock.com/wp-content/uploads/Hardwall-render-Concrete-Half-Length-Composite.png)).

![Innovative materials for advanced earthquake-resistant structures](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_e08ee43a271a4d7d91686a0bfbb8194b~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_62,h_50,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_e08ee43a271a4d7d91686a0bfbb8194b~mv2.png)

Structural resilience will be significantly increased by combining cutting-edge technologies to increase earthquake resistance with innovative materials, especially in harsh weather circumstances.

#### Below is a list of some examples of these developments (Fig. 11):

[**Levitating Houses:**](https://stroyka.md/en/news/v-yaponii-sozdali-doma-paryashchie-v-vozdukhe-2) A Japanese system uses an airbag to lift a house 3cm during earthquakes, protecting it from damage ([Fig. 11a](https://stroyka.md/en/news/v-yaponii-sozdali-doma-paryashchie-v-vozdukhe-2)).

[**Sou Fujimoto Architects:**](https://www.archdaily.com/963611/sou-fujimoto-architects-reveals-vanishing-design-of-qianhais-new-city-center-landmark)Sou Fujimoto Architects' floating tower design in Shenzhen's Qianhaiwan Bay showcases the future of earthquake-resistant structures with its innovative use of materials and resilient engineering ([Fig. 11b](https://www.archdaily.com/963611/sou-fujimoto-architects-reveals-vanishing-design-of-qianhais-new-city-center-landmark)).

[**Komatsu Seiren Fabric Laboratory Fa-Bo:**](https://kkaa.co.jp/en/project/komatsu-seiren-fabric-laboratory-fa-bo/) An office building was renovated into the Fab Labo museum, using carbon fiber to enhance quake resistance. A regional rope braiding technique added flexibility to the carbon fiber ([Fig. 11c](https://kkaa.co.jp/en/project/komatsu-seiren-fabric-laboratory-fa-bo/)).

![Advanced earthquake-resistant structures incorporating innovative technologies](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/a08c91_7e5e36393d1b4f56967ad40b9847e7f3~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_47,h_12,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/a08c91_7e5e36393d1b4f56967ad40b9847e7f3~mv2.png)

As research continues, the potential for innovative smart structures equipped with sensors and adaptive materials is becoming increasingly feasible. This could enable buildings to not only resist earthquakes more effectively but also communicate real-time data to engineers for maintenance and repair.

## **Conclusion**

The adoption of innovative materials in seismic-resistant structures is a vital step toward ensuring the safety and resilience of infrastructure in earthquake-prone regions.

HPFRC, ECC, and 3DCP are transforming the way buildings are designed, offering superior ductility, energy absorption, and crack resistance. As the construction industry evolves, these materials are likely to become integral components of seismic design.

By overcoming the challenges of cost and specialized knowledge, and continuing to explore future advancements like smart materials, we can build more sustainable and earthquake-resilient cities. As seismic activity increases, the role of new materials in protecting lives and reducing structural damage becomes more critical than ever.

URL: https://design.resilience.nz/assets/Documents/Images/seismic-isolation-cover.png
DESCRIPTION: The cover features a modern building with geometric lines in shades of maroon, alongside logos of organizations like nzsee, NZCS, EQC, and Quake Centre, highlighting updated materials and seismic resilience design codes.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://engineeringinfohub.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/antiearthquakestructure_thumb.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts three types of seismic design structures: an earthquake-resistant structure, a damping structure, and a seismic isolation structure, each labeled and showing how they function to enhance stability during earthquakes.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.gcelab.com/assets/images/building-codes-02_1703605887.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The illustration contrasts a structurally sound building with one that has collapsed due to an earthquake, emphasizing the importance of building codes for safety in earthquake-prone areas.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.technostructacademy.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/earthquake-resistant-building-1.webp
DESCRIPTION: The design features illustrations of earthquake-resistant buildings, highlighting three key technologies: seismic resistance, vibration control, and base isolation, accompanied by the title "Earthquake Resistant Buildings: Engineering Resilience for Safety and Sustainability."

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://theconstructor.org/wp-content/uploads/2011/10/earthquake-resistant-design.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The illustration compares fixed-base and base-isolated building designs, highlighting how isolation bearings mitigate ground movement effects during an earthquake.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Advanced materials like High-Performance Fiber-Reinforced Concrete (HPFRC) and Engineered Cementitious Composites (ECC) enhance structural resilience by improving ductility and energy absorption during seismic events.
Seismic design codes now incorporate innovative techniques such as seismic isolation and energy dissipation systems, allowing buildings to absorb and mitigate earthquake forces effectively.
Modern building codes significantly improve safety by mandating the use of advanced materials and construction methods, reducing the likelihood of structural failure during earthquakes.
